risks.title: Inappropriate user access is retained after role change or termination
risks.description: Users retain access rights beyond the period justified by their role, either because access is not revoked when employment ends or because role changes do not trigger a review of the access that is no longer required. The residual access may be used, intentionally or otherwise, to perform actions the user is no longer authorised to perform.

risks.title: Unauthorised changes reach the production environment
risks.description: Changes to application code, configuration, or infrastructure reach production without appropriate approval, testing, or segregation of duties. Unauthorised changes may introduce defects, weaken controls, or permit fraud.

risks.title: Data loss cannot be recovered within the time the business can tolerate
risks.description: Backups are not taken, not tested, not stored securely, or take longer to restore than the business's recovery time objective. A destructive incident leaves the organisation unable to resume operations in the required window.

risks.title: Transaction records are fabricated, manipulated, or otherwise do not reflect genuine business activity
risks.description: Transactional data processed by the application does not faithfully represent underlying business events. This may arise from manual entry of fictitious transactions, adjustment postings made to achieve reporting thresholds, or bulk loading of generated records that bypass normal edit checks. Populations that deviate materially from expected statistical properties (for example, Benford's Law for naturally occurring monetary amounts) may indicate manipulation warranting investigation.

controls.title: Access is provisioned on the basis of a documented, approved request
controls.description: A user's application access is created only on the basis of a request that identifies the user, the role or profile to be granted, and the approver. The request is retained and can be produced for any given account.
controls.objective: Prevent the creation of application access without an identifiable business owner authorising it.

controls.title: User access is reviewed on a recurring basis and exceptions are remediated
controls.description: Management performs a periodic review of application users and their assigned access. The reviewer confirms that each account is still required and that the access granted is still appropriate to the user's role. Exceptions are tracked to closure.
controls.objective: Detect and correct residual access that is no longer justified by the user's role.

controls.title: Changes to production are approved in advance by an authorised approver
controls.description: Every change to production requires documented approval from an authorised approver prior to deployment. The approval identifies the change, the approver, and the date. Emergency changes follow a documented exception path with retrospective review.
controls.objective: Prevent deployment of changes that have not been reviewed and authorised.

controls.title: The developer of a change cannot unilaterally deploy it to production
controls.description: Segregation of duties is enforced between change development and change deployment. No single individual can both author a change and release it to production without a second individual's involvement, whether by system-enforced role separation or a documented, monitored compensating control.
controls.objective: Prevent an individual from bypassing review by deploying their own changes.

controls.title: Backups are taken on a defined schedule and their restoration is tested
controls.description: Backups of the in-scope systems are taken at the frequency defined in the backup policy, stored in a location separate from the primary system, and test-restored on a recurring basis. Restoration test results are retained, including any exceptions and their remediation.
controls.objective: Demonstrate that the organisation can recover from a destructive incident within its declared recovery time objective.

controls.title: Transaction populations are subjected to analytical plausibility testing
controls.description: The application owner or internal audit function runs analytical procedures over transaction populations that are expected to follow known statistical properties. Deviations from the expected distribution are investigated and either explained by a plausible business cause or escalated for fraud review. Results and follow-up actions are retained.
controls.objective: Detect transaction populations whose distribution is inconsistent with naturally occurring business activity, so that material manipulation or fabrication can be investigated before it influences financial reporting.

test_procedures.name: Access provisioning traceability
test_procedures.objective: For a sample of accounts created during the period, confirm that each was created on the basis of a documented, approved request identifying the user, the role, and the approver.
steps: Obtain the complete list of user accounts created in the in-scope application during the audit period.
Select a sample in line with the engagement's sampling approach (default: attribute sampling).
For each sampled account, obtain the provisioning request and approval evidence.
Inspect each to confirm the user identity, role granted, and approver are present and that the approver is authorised to approve access to the relevant role.
For any sampled account where a request or approval cannot be produced, or where the approver is not authorised, record an exception.

items: Complete list of accounts created during the period, extracted from the application.
Provisioning request for each sampled account, including requested role and requester identity.
Approval record for each sampled account, including approver identity and date.
Authorised approver list for the role(s) in question, in effect during the period.

test_procedures.name: User access review completeness and remediation
test_procedures.objective: Confirm that user access reviews were performed at the stated frequency, covered the full population of users at the review date, and that exceptions raised were tracked to remediation.
steps: Obtain the list of access reviews performed during the period and the underlying population of users reviewed in each.
Reconcile the population reviewed to the application's user list as at the review date.
Inspect the reviewer's sign-off evidence for each review.
For a sample of exceptions raised during the reviews, obtain evidence that each was remediated or formally accepted.
Record an exception where a review was missed, a portion of the population was omitted, or an exception was not remediated within the stated remediation window.

items: Access review report or sign-off for each review in the period.
User list extracted from the application as at the review date, for reconciliation.
Exception log with status and remediation evidence for each exception raised.

test_procedures.name: Review of dormant application accounts
test_procedures.objective: Confirm that enabled accounts whose last sign-in is older than the organisation's defined activity window have either been disabled or formally justified as retained.
steps: Obtain an AD (or Entra) export of application accounts as at the review date, including enablement status and last sign-in timestamp.
Confirm the organisation's defined dormancy threshold (commonly 90 days for standard users, shorter for privileged roles).
Run the dormant-accounts matcher against the export; it flags every enabled account whose last sign-in is older than the threshold, plus accounts that have never signed in.

For each flagged account, obtain either evidence that the account has since been disabled, or the business justification for retaining it (for example: service accounts, leave of absence, shared operational mailboxes).
Record an exception where an account is flagged, remains enabled, and has no accepted justification.

items: AD or Entra export of application accounts as at the review date, including enablement status and last sign-in timestamp.
Documented dormancy threshold in the organisation's access management policy, with role-based variants if any.
Disablement evidence or documented business justification for each flagged account.

test_procedures.name: Review of orphan application accounts
test_procedures.objective: Confirm that enabled application accounts held by individuals who are not current employees have been disabled or formally justified as retained.
steps: Obtain an AD (or Entra) export of application accounts as at the review date, including enablement status and identifier columns (email or logon name).
Obtain the HR master list of current employees as at the same review date, including the same identifier columns used for matching.
Confirm that the HR master excludes terminated employees (those belong on the leavers list consumed by the terminated-but-active matcher, not here).
Run the orphan-accounts matcher; it flags every enabled AD account whose identifier does not appear anywhere in the HR master.

For each flagged account, obtain evidence of one of: (a) disablement; (b) formal classification as a service or shared account with a named owner; (c) a documented justification for retaining it despite the gap in HR records.
Record an exception where an account remains enabled with no HR record and no accepted classification or justification.

items: AD or Entra export of application accounts as at the review date, including enablement status.
HR master list of current employees as at the review date, with the same identifier columns used for matching (email and/or logon name).
Service-account register or equivalent classification record, if relied on to exclude non-employee accounts.
Disablement evidence or documented business justification for each flagged account not classified as a service account.

test_procedures.name: Change approval before production deployment
test_procedures.objective: For a sample of production changes deployed during the period, confirm that each was approved in advance by an authorised approver.
steps: Obtain the complete list of production changes deployed during the audit period.
Select a sample in line with the engagement's sampling approach.
For each sampled change, obtain the approval record and the deployment record.
Inspect each to confirm the approval was granted before the deployment timestamp and that the approver is on the authorised approver list for the change type and environment.
For emergency changes, additionally inspect the retrospective review record.
Record an exception where approval was absent, granted after deployment, or granted by an unauthorised approver.

items: Complete list of production changes deployed in the period, with deployment timestamps.
Change approval record for each sampled change.
Authorised approver list for the period, by environment or change type.
Retrospective review record for each sampled emergency change, if applicable.

test_procedures.name: Segregation of duties between development and deployment
test_procedures.objective: Confirm that no individual can both author a change and deploy it to production, either through system-enforced role separation or through a documented, monitored compensating control.
steps: Obtain the production deployment tool's role or permission matrix.
Identify the population of users with deploy-to-production permission.
Identify the population of users with write access to the source repository or change authoring tool.
Compute the intersection; for each user in the intersection, obtain evidence of the compensating control (e.g. four-eyes review, post-deployment monitoring) and its operation during the period.
Record an exception where an individual had both permissions and no compensating control was evidenced, or where the compensating control did not operate consistently.

items: Production deployment tool's permission export, including role assignments.
Source repository or change authoring tool's access export.
Documented compensating control, where relied on, and evidence of its operation during the period.

test_procedures.name: Backup performance and restoration testing
test_procedures.objective: Confirm that backups were taken on schedule during the period, stored in a separate location, and test-restored with results retained.
steps: Obtain the backup schedule and the backup job log for the period.
Inspect the log to confirm each scheduled backup either completed successfully or was followed by a rerun that completed.
For a sample of backup dates, inspect evidence that the backup was stored in the separate location stated in the backup policy.
Obtain the restoration test log for the period and the retained test results.
For each restoration test in the sample, inspect evidence that the test was performed, whether it succeeded, and the remediation of any failure.
Record an exception where a scheduled backup failed without a successful rerun, a backup was not stored per policy, or a restoration test was skipped or failed without remediation.

items: Backup policy in effect during the period, including schedule and storage location.
Backup job log covering the full period.
Evidence of storage location for sampled backup dates.
Restoration test log and retained restoration test results, with remediation evidence for any failed test.

test_procedures.name: Benford's Law first-digit analysis on transaction population
test_procedures.objective: Assess whether the distribution of first digits across the in-scope transaction population is consistent with Benford's Law. A material deviation, measured by a per-digit frequency threshold and a chi-square goodness-of-fit statistic against the 8-df critical value at α=0.05, indicates a population that warrants investigation for manipulation, bulk generation, or threshold-driven rounding.
steps: Obtain an export of the in-scope transaction population covering the audit period, including a monetary amount column. Confirm the population is whole — no sampling at this stage; Benford is a population-level test.

Confirm that the population is plausibly Benford-applicable: amounts span at least two orders of magnitude, are not bounded within a narrow range (for example, fixed-fee products), and are not rounded to a small set of internal values.

Run the Benford first-digit matcher against the export; it computes the observed frequency for each leading digit 1..9, compares to the expected Benford frequencies, and reports the chi-square statistic with a pass/fail against the 15.507 critical value.

For each digit the matcher flags as materially deviating from Benford, investigate the underlying transactions: obtain a sample of records contributing to the over- or under-represented digit and identify whether a plausible business cause explains the skew (for example: a product at a single price point, a posting threshold, a rounding rule, or a currency conversion).
Where no plausible cause explains the deviation, escalate the population to internal audit or fraud review per the organisation's policy, and document the response.
Record an exception where the population fails the test and no plausible cause or retained investigation record explains the deviation.

items: Complete export of the in-scope transaction population for the audit period, with a monetary amount column.
Description of the population's expected distribution, including documented rounding or threshold rules that could bias first digits.
Matcher run report, including chi-square statistic, per-digit observed versus expected frequencies, and the list of flagged digits.
For any flagged digit: management's explanation and a supporting sample of records, or evidence of escalation to internal audit or fraud review.

test_procedures.name: Duplicate-transaction detection on transaction population
test_procedures.objective: Identify groups of transactions that share the same monetary amount, counterparty, and date. Exact duplicates on this triple are unusual in genuine business activity and warrant investigation as potential double-postings, duplicated invoices, or fabricated records.
steps: Obtain a complete export of the in-scope transaction population covering the audit period, including columns for monetary amount, counterparty (vendor, customer, or account name), and transaction date.
Confirm the population is whole — no sampling at this stage; duplicate detection is a population-level test.

Run the duplicate-transaction matcher against the export. It groups rows by the tuple (amount, counterparty, date) using normalised comparison (case-insensitive, whitespace-collapsed counterparty; integer-cents amount; date string in the form provided by the source) and reports every group containing two or more rows.

For each flagged group, obtain the underlying source documents (invoice, receipt, journal entry) and evidence of the business reason for the repeated posting, or evidence of a reversal or correction if one of the rows was posted in error.
Where no plausible business reason explains the repetition, escalate the group to internal audit or accounts-payable review per the organisation's policy and document the response.
Record an exception where a flagged group has no plausible business explanation and no retained investigation record.

items: Complete export of the in-scope transaction population for the audit period, with columns for monetary amount, counterparty, and date.
Matcher run report, including the number of duplicate groups, total duplicate rows, and per-group amount, counterparty, date, and row ordinals.
For each flagged group: underlying source documents for each row, and management's explanation or evidence of reversal, correction, or investigation.

test_procedures.name: Boundary / threshold analysis on transaction population
test_procedures.objective: Identify transactions clustered just below known approval thresholds. Populations with materially more rows in the just-below window than in the just-above window indicate the threshold may be gamed — transactions split, timed, or priced to dodge the authorisation rule.
steps: Obtain a complete export of the in-scope transaction population covering the audit period, including a monetary amount column.
Confirm the population is whole — no sampling at this stage; boundary analysis is a population-level test.

Run the boundary-threshold matcher against the export. It evaluates a fixed list of common approval thresholds in the population's currency and, for each threshold T, counts rows falling in a just-below window [T - W, T) and a just-above window [T, T + W], where W is 5% of T. A threshold is flagged when the below window holds at least 10 rows AND the below-to-above ratio is at least 2.0 (a below window with zero above-rows is treated as unbounded ratio and flagged on the absolute count alone).

For each flagged threshold, obtain from management either (a) the organisation's documented approval matrix showing the threshold in question is not an authorisation boundary for the transaction class, or (b) the business explanation for the cluster (for example: a product at a price point just below the threshold, a fixed-fee schedule, or a seasonal bulk posting), or (c) evidence of investigation and escalation for gaming.
Where no plausible cause or retained investigation explains the cluster, escalate the threshold to internal audit or fraud review per the organisation's policy and document the response.
Record an exception where a flagged threshold has no plausible business explanation and no retained investigation record.

items: Complete export of the in-scope transaction population for the audit period, with a monetary amount column.
Organisation's documented approval matrix, showing monetary thresholds that require higher-level authorisation by transaction class.

Matcher run report, including the thresholds evaluated, thresholds flagged, and for each flagged threshold the below-window count, above-window count, below/above ratio, and a sample of contributing rows.
For each flagged threshold: management's business explanation and supporting evidence, or evidence of escalation to internal audit or fraud review.

test_procedures.name: Recurring-amount detection across counterparties
test_procedures.objective: Identify monetary amounts that recur with unusual frequency across multiple unrelated counterparties. Genuine business activity rarely produces the same amount paid to or received from many distinct parties; clusters with that signature warrant investigation as potential template-driven postings, kickback patterns, structured payments below internal thresholds, or fabricated records assembled from a single placeholder figure.
steps: Obtain a complete export of the in-scope transaction population covering the audit period, including columns for monetary amount and counterparty (vendor, customer, or account name).

Confirm the population is whole — no sampling at this stage; recurring-amount detection is a population-level test. Confirm the population is plausibly varied: a fixed-fee product schedule will naturally cluster amounts at single price points and is not the target of this rule.

Run the recurring-amount matcher against the export. It groups rows by absolute monetary amount (integer cents) and, for each amount group, counts the number of distinct normalised counterparties. A group is flagged when it covers at least 5 distinct counterparties AND holds at least 5 rows AND the amount itself is at least the significance floor (default $100 in the population's currency) — small repeating figures and single-counterparty repetitions are out of scope here.

For each flagged amount, obtain from management either (a) the documented business reason for the repetition (for example: a uniform service fee, a regulatory levy, a recurring subscription priced identically), or (b) the underlying source documents for a sample of contributing rows so the auditor can corroborate that distinct goods or services were received from each counterparty, or (c) evidence of investigation and escalation for fabrication or structuring.
Where no plausible business reason explains the cluster, escalate the amount to internal audit or fraud review per the organisation's policy and document the response.
Record an exception where a flagged amount has no plausible business explanation and no retained investigation record.

items: Complete export of the in-scope transaction population for the audit period, with columns for monetary amount and counterparty.

Description of the population's expected pricing model, including any uniform fee schedule, recurring subscription pricing, or regulatory levy that would legitimately produce identical amounts across counterparties.
Matcher run report, including the recurring-group count, total recurring rows, and per-group amount, distinct counterparty count, sample counterparties, and contributing row ordinals.

For each flagged amount: management's business explanation and supporting evidence, or underlying source documents for a sample of contributing rows, or evidence of escalation to internal audit or fraud review.
